docs.title: SE2SE Introduction to k8s
docs.text:
This hands-on Scenario series is designed to build practical familiarity with the core concepts of Kubernetes (k8s) through guided, step-by-step exercises.

The workshop is based on KinD (Kubernetes-in-Docker), a lightweight and accessible way to run Kubernetes clusters locally. KinD provides an ideal environment for learning and experimentation, allowing you to create, manage, and reset clusters quickly without requiring a full production infrastructure.

The primary objective of these Scenarios is to ensure participants become comfortable with foundational Kubernetes building blocks, including:

  • Kubernetes manifests (YAML-based configuration)
  • Pods and Deployments
  • Services and external connectivity
  • Networking fundamentals and Network Policies

These concepts directly relate to modern cloud-native application delivery and security, and they provide the groundwork for understanding Cisco's Cloud Protection Suite, including Isovalent and its ecosystem.

All Scenarios are delivered in a pre-configured Cisco dCloud environment, where required tools and dependencies are already installed. This allows you to focus entirely on the hands-on tasks rather than setup or troubleshooting.

Keywords: k8s, kind, cilium, cni, docker, compose, container, pods, deployment, helm, kubectl.

docs.title: Scenario 2: Deploying Apps in Kubernetes with KinD
Scenario 3: Network Isolation with Calico in Kubernetes
Scenario 4: Advanced Networking with Cilium CNI
Scenario 4: Advanced Networking with Cilium CNI in Kubernetes
docs.text:
In this lab, you will install the Cilium CNI into a KinD cluster using Helm and learn how Cilium extends Kubernetes networking beyond what traditional CNIs like Calico offer. Cilium leverages eBPF (extended Berkeley Packet Filter) to provide high-performance networking, observability, and security—including L7 (application-layer) policy enforcement.

You will create a cluster without a default CNI and without kube-proxy, install Cilium via Helm, enable Hubble for real-time network observability, build a complete network policy stack that includes both standard L3/L4 and Cilium-native L7 HTTP policies, and then expose your secured application to the outside world using LoadBalancer services with L2 announcements.

docs.title: Goals
docs.text:
By the end of this lab, you will be able to:

  • Create a KinD cluster without a default CNI and without kube-proxy
  • Install Cilium CNI using Helm (and understand the difference vs. static manifests)
  • Verify Cilium and Hubble are operational
  • Access the Hubble UI from your remote workstation
  • Apply Kubernetes NetworkPolicies (deny-all, allow-dns, allow-http)
  • Apply Cilium L7 HTTP-aware policies using CiliumNetworkPolicy
  • Apply Cilium FQDN-based egress policies
  • Configure L2 announcements and LoadBalancer services to expose secured workloads
  • Use Hubble CLI and UI to observe and debug policy enforcement in real time

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
Before starting this lab, you should have:

  • Completed Labs 0–2
  • Docker image: lab1-express:1.0 (from Lab 1)
  • Familiarity with Kubernetes NetworkPolicies (Lab 3)
  • Cilium CLI and Hubble CLI installed (verified in Lab 0, Tasks 5.1 and 5.2)

Verify Cilium CLI:

cilium version

Verify Hubble CLI:

hubble version

Verify the lab1-express image is available:

docker images --format "{{.Repository}}:{{.Tag}}" | grep lab1-express

The --format "{{.Repository}}:{{.Tag}}" is a helper to filter for specific fields in the Docker API. if you use docker image | grep lab1 instead, you will get a warning

docs.title: Workspace
docs.text:
KinD cluster configuration:

~/workspace/kind/lab4/

Kubernetes and Cilium manifests:

~/workspace/k8s/lab4/

docs.title: Tasks
docs.text:
The diagram is a summary of all the elements we are going to use during this lab.

flowchart TB subgraph kind["KinD Cluster — no kube-proxy"] subgraph cilium["Cilium CNI (eBPF)"] direction TB kpr("Kube-Proxy \nReplacement") l2("L2 Announcements") np("Network Policies L3/L4") cnp("Cilium \nNetwork Policy L7") end subgraph hubble["Hubble Observability"] relay{{"Hubble Relay"}} ui{{"Hubble UI"}} end subgraph workloads["Workloads"] nginxa(nginx-a) nginxb(nginx-b) express(lab1-express) end cilium --> workloads hubble --> cilium end browser("Jumphost Browser") -->|port-forward| ui host("Host curl") -->|LoadBalancer IP| express classDef k8s_nodes padding: 8px,fill:#0a60ff,color:#e7e7e7, stroke:#07182d,stroke-width:2px, font-size:1.25em; classDef k8s_cilium padding: 8px,fill:#02c8ff,color:#07182d, stroke:#02c8ff,stroke-width:2px, font-size:1.2em; classDef k8s_ns padding: 16px,stroke:#07182d,stroke-width:4px, font-size:1.1em; class workloads,hubble,cilium,kind k8s_ns; class kpr,np,l2,cnp,relay,ui k8s_cilium; class browser,host,nginxa,nginxb,express k8s_nodes;

docs.title: Task 1: Create a KinD Cluster without CNI and without kube-proxy
docs.text: In Lab 3, you created a cluster with disableDefaultCNI: true to install Calico. In this lab, you will also disable kube-proxy so that Cilium can fully replace it using eBPF-based packet processing. This is known as kube-proxy replacement and is the recommended deployment model for Cilium.

docs.title: 1.1 Create the KinD configuration folder
docs.text:
mkdir -p ~/workspace/kind/lab4
cd ~/workspace/kind/lab4

docs.title: 1.2 Create the KinD cluster configuration file
docs.text:
Create a file named kind-cilium-config.yaml:

kind: Cluster
apiVersion: kind.x-k8s.io/v1alpha4
nodes:
  - role: control-plane
    extraPortMappings:
      - containerPort: 30000
        hostPort: 30000
      - containerPort: 31100
        hostPort: 31100
      - containerPort: 31200
        hostPort: 31200
      - containerPort: 31300
        hostPort: 31300
      - containerPort: 31400
        hostPort: 31400
      - containerPort: 31500
        hostPort: 31500
  - role: worker
  - role: worker
networking:
  disableDefaultCNI: true
  kubeProxyMode: "none"

Key differences from Lab 3:

  • disableDefaultCNI: true — same as before, prevents KinD from installing the default kindnet CNI
  • kubeProxyMode: "none" — new: disables kube-proxy entirely, allowing Cilium to handle all service routing via eBPF

docs.title: 1.3 Create the cluster
docs.text:
kind create cluster --name my-k8s --config kind-cilium-config.yaml

Expected Result:

Creating cluster "my-k8s" ...
Set kubectl context to "kind-my-k8s"

docs.title: 1.4 Verify the cluster nodes are Not Ready
docs.text:
kubectl get nodes -o wide

Expected Result:

All nodes should show NotReady status — this is expected because no CNI is installed yet.

docs.title: 1.5 Verify kube-proxy is NOT running
docs.text:
kubectl get pods -n kube-system

Expected Result:

You should not see any kube-proxy pods. You will see coredns pods in Pending state (waiting for the CNI).

Kube-Proxy is a Kubernetes agent installed on every node in the cluster. Kube-proxy implement mapping "Service to Pod". When traffic reaches the Service, it is redirected to the backend Pods accordingly.

docs.title: Task 2: Install Cilium CNI using Helm
docs.text: In Lab 3, Calico was installed using a static YAML manifest (kubectl apply -f calico.yaml). In this lab, you will install Cilium using Helm, which is the recommended installation method.

docs.title: Manifests vs. Helm Charts
docs.text:
Aspect Static Manifest (kubectl apply -f) Helm Chart (helm install) Configuration Edit YAML directly Use --set flags or values.yaml Upgrades Re-download and re-apply manually helm upgrade with version control Rollback Manual helm rollback built-in Customization Search-and-replace in large YAML Templated, parameterized values Reproducibility Hard to track what changed Version-pinned, declarative Best for Quick demos, simple installs Production, repeatable environments

Helm is a package manager for Kubernetes. A Helm chart is a collection of templates and default values that produce Kubernetes manifests. Think of it as docker compose but for Kubernetes resources.

docs.title: 2.1 Add the Cilium Helm repository
docs.text:
helm repo add cilium https://helm.cilium.io/
helm repo update

Expected Result:

"cilium" has been added to your repositories
...Successfully got an update from the "cilium" chart repository

docs.title: 2.2 Install Cilium with Helm
docs.text:
helm install cilium cilium/cilium \
  --namespace kube-system \
  --set kubeProxyReplacement=true \
  --set k8sServiceHost=172.18.0.2 \
  --set k8sServicePort=6443 \
  --set hubble.enabled=true \
  --set hubble.relay.enabled=true \
  --set hubble.ui.enabled=true \
  --set l2announcements.enabled=true \
  --set externalIPs.enabled=true \
  --wait --timeout 5m

Explanation of key flags:

  • kubeProxyReplacement=true — Cilium fully replaces kube-proxy using eBPF
  • k8sServiceHost / k8sServicePort — tells Cilium how to reach the API server (required when kube-proxy is disabled). In the case of KinD cluster this should be the IP address of the control plane node, you can get it running kubectl get nodes -o wide
  • hubble.enabled=true — enables the Hubble observability layer
  • hubble.relay.enabled=true — enables the Hubble Relay (required for the UI and CLI)
  • hubble.ui.enabled=true — deploys the Hubble web UI
  • l2announcements.enabled=true — enables L2 (ARP) announcements for LoadBalancer services (used in Task 11)
  • externalIPs.enabled=true — allows Cilium to handle external IP assignments (used in Task 11)

Expected Result:

Helm will install all Cilium components and wait for them to be ready.

docs.title: 2.3 Verify Cilium pods are running
docs.text:
cilium status --wait

Expected Result:

You should see several pods running:

  • cilium-* (one per node — DaemonSet)
  • cilium-operator-*
  • hubble-relay-*
  • hubble-ui-*

docs.title: 2.4 Verify nodes are Ready
docs.text:
kubectl get nodes -o wide

Expected Result:

All nodes should now show Ready status.

docs.title: Task 3: Verify Hubble and Access the Hubble UI
docs.text: Hubble provides deep visibility into the network traffic flowing through your cluster. In this task, you will verify Hubble is working and access the Hubble UI from your remote workstation.

docs.title: 3.1 Verify Hubble status
docs.text:
cilium hubble port-forward &
hubble status

Expected Result:

Healtcheck (via localhost:4245): Ok

docs.title: 3.2 Observe live traffic with Hubble CLI
docs.text:
hubble observe --last 10

This will show the most recent 10 network flows observed by Hubble across the cluster.

Expected Result:

You should see flows related to Cilium health checks and CoreDNS activity.

docs.title: 3.3 Access the Hubble UI via kubectl port-forward
docs.text:
To access the Hubble web UI from the Jumphost browser, you need to do a kubectl port forwarding in a terminal window:

kubectl port-forward -n kube-system svc/hubble-ui 12000:80 --address 0.0.0.0 &

Note: The --address 0.0.0.0 flag makes the forwarded port accessible from remote machines (the Jumphost), not just localhost.

IMPORTANT Keep this terminal window open for the rest of the lab, because the port-forward is dependant on the terminal session. This can be fixed with tools like tmux, but it is beyond the scope of the lab. If you lost the connectivity, just open a new terminal session and redo the forwarding.

From the Jumphost browser, navigate to:

http://198.18.128.50:12000

Tip: You can use the bookmark named Hubble UI in Chrome for quick access during the rest of the lab.

Expected Result:

The Hubble UI loads and shows a service map of your cluster. At this point, you will only see system namespaces (e.g., kube-system).

docs.title: Task 4: Deploy Sample Workloads
docs.text:
In this task, you will deploy workloads across two namespaces, similar to Lab 3, but this time you will also deploy the Lab 1 Express application to demonstrate L7 policy enforcement later.

flowchart TD subgraph node["KinD Cluster"] subgraph demo-b["namespace: demo-b"] pod-b(nginx-b) end subgraph demo-a["namespace: demo-a"] pod-a(nginx-a) pod-express(lab1-express) end end classDef k8s_nodes padding: 8px,fill:#0a60ff,color:#e7e7e7, stroke:#07182d,stroke-width:2px, font-size:1.25em; classDef k8s_cilium padding: 8px,fill:#02c8ff,color:#07182d, stroke:#02c8ff,stroke-width:2px, font-size:1.2em; classDef k8s_ns padding: 16px,stroke:#07182d,stroke-width:4px, font-size:1.1em; class demo-a,demo-b k8s_ns; class pod-a,pod-b,pod-express k8s_nodes;

docs.title: 4.1 Create namespaces
docs.text:
Create workloads folder in ~/workspace/k8s/lab4

Then create demo-ns.yaml file with this content

apiVersion: v1
kind: Namespace
metadata:
  name: demo-a
  labels:
    name: demo-a

---
apiVersion: v1
kind: Namespace
metadata:
  name: demo-b
  labels:
    name: demo-b

Run:

cd ~/workspace/k8s/lab4/workloads
kubectl apply -f demo-ns
kubectl get ns --show-labels

Expected Result:

Alternative via kubectl API

kubectl create ns demo-a
kubectl create ns demo-b

Label the namespaces:

kubectl label ns demo-a name=demo-a
kubectl label ns demo-b name=demo-b

docs.title: 4.2 Load the Lab 1 Express image into KinD
docs.text:
This is required just because the labs use KinD and the lab1-express:1.0 image doesn't live in a registry, so this is a "manual copy" of the container image into the kind cluster

kind load docker-image lab1-express:1.0 --name my-k8s

docs.title: 4.3 Deploy NGINX in each namespace
docs.text:
kubectl create deployment nginx-a --image=nginx -n demo-a
kubectl expose deployment nginx-a --port=80 -n demo-a

kubectl create deployment nginx-b --image=nginx -n demo-b
kubectl expose deployment nginx-b --port=80 -n demo-b

docs.title: 4.4 Deploy the Express app in demo-a
docs.text:
Go to the manifest directory:

cd ~/workspace/k8s/lab4/workloads

Create express-deployment.yaml:

apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: lab1-express
  namespace: demo-a
spec:
  replicas: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: lab1-express
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: lab1-express
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: express
          image: lab1-express:1.0
          imagePullPolicy: Never
          ports:
            - containerPort: 3000
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: lab1-express-svc
  namespace: demo-a
spec:
  selector:
    app: lab1-express
  ports:
    - port: 3000
      targetPort: 3000

Apply:

kubectl apply -f express-deployment.yaml

docs.title: 4.5 Verify all workloads
docs.text:
kubectl get pods,svc -n demo-a
kubectl get pods,svc -n demo-b

Expected Result:

  • demo-a: nginx-a pod + service, lab1-express pod + service
  • demo-b: nginx-b pod + service

docs.title: Task 5: Confirm Default Connectivity (No Policies)
docs.text:
Before applying any network policies, verify that all pods can communicate freely.

flowchart LR subgraph demo-a["namespace: demo-a"] testpod(Testpod)--✅ Port 80-->nginxa(nginx-a) testpod--✅ Port 3000-->express(lab1-express) end subgraph demo-b["namespace: demo-b"] nginxb(nginx-b) end testpod--✅ Port 80-->nginxb testpod--✅ Port 53-->kube(kube-dns) classDef k8s_nodes padding: 8px,fill:#0a60ff,color:#e7e7e7, stroke:#07182d,stroke-width:2px, font-size:1.25em; classDef k8s_cilium padding: 8px,fill:#02c8ff,color:#07182d, stroke:#02c8ff,stroke-width:2px, font-size:1.2em; classDef k8s_ns padding: 16px,stroke:#07182d,stroke-width:4px, font-size:1.1em; class testpod,nginxb,nginxa,express,kube k8s_nodes; class demo-b,demo-a k8s_ns;

All traffic is open by default — any pod can reach any other pod across all namespaces and ports.

docs.title: 5.1 Test cross-namespace connectivity
docs.text:
kubectl run testpod --rm -ti --image=busybox --restart=Never -n demo-a -- /bin/sh

Inside the busybox shell:

wget -q --timeout=5 nginx-b.demo-b.svc.cluster.local -O -
wget -q --timeout=5 lab1-express-svc.demo-a.svc.cluster.local:3000 -O -

Expected Result:

Both requests succeed — NGINX HTML and Express greeting are returned.

Type exit to leave the test pod.

docs.title: 5.2 Observe default traffic in Hubble
docs.text:
Before applying any policies, check what Hubble sees:

hubble observe --namespace demo-a --last 10

Open the Hubble UI in Chrome and select demo-a from the namespace dropdown. You should see all flows as green (forwarded) — no drops yet. This is your baseline.

docs.title: Network Policies with Cilium
docs.text:
Cilium supports both standard Kubernetes NetworkPolicies (L3/L4) and its own CiliumNetworkPolicy CRD which adds L7 (HTTP, gRPC, Kafka) policy enforcement.

In this section you will build the same policy stack from Lab 3, and then extend it with Cilium-native L7 and FQDN policies.

flowchart TB subgraph demo-a["namespace: demo-a"] subgraph k8s-policies["Standard k8s NetworkPolicies (L3/L4)"] P1("🔴 deny-all podSelector: {} Ingress + Egress") P2("🟢 allow-dns-egress podSelector: {} Egress → :53") P3("🔵 allow-nginx-a-ingress podSelector: nginx-a Ingress ← :80") P4("🟢 allow-http-egress podSelector: {} Egress → :80/:3000") end subgraph cilium-policies["CiliumNetworkPolicy (L3/L4 + L7)"] P5("🟣 l7-express-policy Ingress to lab1-express Allow GET / and GET /health only") end end subgraph demo-b["namespace: demo-b"] subgraph cilium-policies-b["CiliumNetworkPolicy (FQDN)"] P6("🟠 l7-allow-google-egress Egress to google.com only") end end classDef k8s_nodes padding: 8px,fill:#0a60ff,color:#e7e7e7, stroke:#07182d,stroke-width:2px, font-size:1.25em; classDef k8s_cilium padding: 8px,fill:#02c8ff,color:#07182d, stroke:#02c8ff,stroke-width:2px, font-size:1.25em; classDef k8s_ns padding: 16px,stroke:#07182d,stroke-width:4px, font-size:1.1em; class k8s-policies,cilium-policies,cilium-policies-b,demo-b,demo-a k8s_ns; class P1,P2,P3,P4,P5,P6 k8s_nodes;

Important: Throughout this section, you will use both hubble observe on the CLI and the Hubble UI in Chrome to validate each policy. This builds familiarity with Hubble as a real-time debugging tool — not just something you check at the end.

docs.title: Task 6: Apply a Default Deny Policy
docs.text: Same as Lab 3, start with a deny-all baseline.

docs.title: 6.1 Create the policies directory
docs.text:
mkdir -p ~/workspace/k8s/lab4/policies
cd ~/workspace/k8s/lab4/policies

docs.title: 6.2 Create a deny-all policy
docs.text:
Create default-deny-all.yaml:

apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: default-deny-all
  namespace: demo-a
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  policyTypes:
    - Ingress
    - Egress

Apply:

kubectl apply -f deny-all.yaml

docs.title: 6.3 Verify the policy
docs.text: kubectl describe networkpolicy default-deny-all -n demo-a

docs.title: 6.4 Test connectivity after deny-all
docs.text:
flowchart LR subgraph demo-a["namespace: demo-a"] testpod(Testpod)--Egress Port 80-->P1((❌))--Deny-->nginxa(nginx-a) testpod--Egress Port 3000-->P2((❌))--Deny-->express(lab1-express) end subgraph demo-b["namespace: demo-b"] nginxb(nginx-b) end subgraph kube-ns["namespace: kube-system"] kube(kube-dns) end testpod--Egress Port 80-->P3((❌))--Deny-->nginxb testpod--Egress Port 53-->P4((❌))--Deny-->kube(kube-dns) classDef policy_deny color:#fff,stroke:#EB4651,stroke-width:2px; classDef policy_allow color:#fff,stroke:#45991f,stroke-width:2px; classDef k8s_nodes padding: 8px,fill:#0a60ff,color:#e7e7e7, stroke:#07182d,stroke-width:2px, font-size:1.25em; classDef k8s_ns padding: 16px,stroke:#07182d,stroke-width:4px, font-size:1.1em; class P1,P2,P3,P4 policy_deny; class testpod,nginxb,nginxa,express,kube k8s_nodes; class kube-ns,demo-a,demo-b k8s_ns;

After deny-all, every connection attempt is blocked — including DNS resolution.

kubectl run testpod --rm -ti --image=busybox --restart=Never -n demo-a -- /bin/sh

Inside the shell:

nslookup nginx-a.demo-a.svc.cluster.local
wget -qO- --timeout=5 nginx-b.demo-b.svc.cluster.local

Expected Result:

Both DNS resolution and HTTP requests fail — all traffic is blocked.

Type exit to leave the test pod.

docs.title: 6.5 Observe in Hubble
docs.text:
hubble observe --namespace demo-a --verdict DROPPED --last 10

Check the Hubble UI in Chrome — select demo-a and you should now see red (dropped) flows. Every connection attempt from any pod in demo-a is being ❌. Compare this with the all-green baseline from Task 5.

docs.title: Task 7: Allow Egress to DNS Service
7.1 Create the allow-dns policy
docs.text:
Create allow-dns-egress.yaml:

apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: allow-dns-egress
  namespace: demo-a
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  policyTypes:
    - Egress
  egress:
    - to:
        - namespaceSelector:
            matchLabels:
              kubernetes.io/metadata.name: kube-system
      ports:
        - protocol: UDP
          port: 53
        - protocol: TCP
          port: 53

Apply:

kubectl apply -f allow-dns-egress.yaml

docs.title: 7.2 Validate DNS works
docs.text:
flowchart LR subgraph demo-a["namespace: demo-a"] testpod--Egress Port 80-->P2((❌))-->nginxa(nginx-a) testpod--Egress Port 3000-->P3((❌))-->express(lab1-express) end subgraph demo-b["namespace: demo-b"] nginxb(nginx-b) end subgraph kube-ns["namespace: kube-system"] kube(kube-dns) end testpod(Testpod)--Egress Port 53-->P1((✅))--DNS-->kube(kube-dns) testpod--Egress Port 80-->P4((❌))-->nginxb classDef policy_deny color:#fff,stroke:#EB4651,stroke-width:2px; classDef policy_allow color:#fff,stroke:#45991f,stroke-width:2px; classDef k8s_nodes padding: 8px,fill:#0a60ff,color:#e7e7e7, stroke:#07182d,stroke-width:2px, font-size:1.25em; classDef k8s_ns padding: 16px,stroke:#07182d,stroke-width:4px, font-size:1.1em; class P1 policy_allow; class P2,P3,P4 policy_deny; class testpod,nginxb,nginxa,express,kube k8s_nodes; class kube-ns,demo-a,demo-b k8s_ns;

DNS is now allowed, but all HTTP traffic remains blocked.

kubectl run testpod --rm -ti --image=busybox --restart=Never -n demo-a -- /bin/sh

Inside the shell:

nslookup nginx-a.demo-a.svc.cluster.local

Expected Result:

DNS resolution succeeds, but HTTP requests still fail (no egress rule for port 80 yet).

Type exit to leave the test pod.

docs.title: 7.3 Observe in Hubble
docs.text:
hubble observe --namespace demo-a --last 10

You should now see a mix: DNS flows to kube-system show as forwarded (green), while HTTP attempts still show as dropped (red). In the Hubble UI, the connection line to kube-dns turns green while everything else remains red.

docs.title: Task 8: Allow Ingress and HTTP Egress
8.1 Create an ingress policy for nginx-a
docs.text:
Create allow-nginx-a-ingress.yaml:

apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: allow-nginx-a-ingress
  namespace: demo-a
spec:
  podSelector:
    matchLabels:
      app: nginx-a
  policyTypes:
    - Ingress
  ingress:
    - from:
        - namespaceSelector:
            matchLabels:
              kubernetes.io/metadata.name: demo-a
      ports:
        - protocol: TCP
          port: 80

Apply:

kubectl apply -f allow-nginx-a-ingress.yaml

docs.title: 8.2 Create an ingress policy for the Express app
docs.text:
Create allow-express-ingress.yaml:

apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: allow-express-ingress
  namespace: demo-a
spec:
  podSelector:
    matchLabels:
      app: lab1-express
  policyTypes:
    - Ingress
  ingress:
    - from:
        - namespaceSelector:
            matchLabels:
              kubernetes.io/metadata.name: demo-a
      ports:
        - protocol: TCP
          port: 3000

Apply:

kubectl apply -f allow-express-ingress.yaml

docs.title: 8.3 Create an HTTP egress policy
docs.text:
Create allow-http-egress.yaml:

apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: allow-http-egress
  namespace: demo-a
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  policyTypes:
    - Egress
  egress:
    - to:
        - namespaceSelector:
            matchLabels:
              name: demo-a
      ports:
        - protocol: TCP
          port: 80
        - protocol: TCP
          port: 443
        - protocol: TCP
          port: 3000

Apply:

kubectl apply -f allow-http-egress.yaml

docs.title: 8.4 Validate the complete L3/L4 policy stack
docs.text:
flowchart LR subgraph demo-a["namespace: demo-a"] testpod--Egress Port 80-->P2((✅))--Ingress Port 80-->nginxa(nginx-a) testpod--Egress Port 3000-->P3((✅))--Ingress Port 3000-->express(lab1-express) end subgraph demo-b["namespace: demo-b"] nginxb(nginx-b) end subgraph kube-ns["namespace: kube-system"] kube(kube-dns) end testpod(Testpod)--Egress Port 53-->P1((✅))--DNS-->kube(kube-dns) testpod--Egress Port 80-->P4((❌))--demo-b-->nginxb classDef policy_deny color:#fff,stroke:#EB4651,stroke-width:2px; classDef policy_allow color:#fff,stroke:#45991f,stroke-width:2px; classDef k8s_nodes padding: 8px,fill:#0a60ff,color:#e7e7e7, stroke:#07182d,stroke-width:2px, font-size:1.25em; classDef k8s_ns padding: 16px,stroke:#07182d,stroke-width:4px, font-size:1.1em; class P4 policy_deny; class P1,P2,P3 policy_allow; class testpod,nginxb,nginxa,express,kube k8s_nodes; class kube-ns,demo-a,demo-b k8s_ns;

DNS, nginx-a, and lab1-express within demo-a are all reachable. Cross-namespace traffic to demo-b remains blocked.

kubectl run testpod --rm -ti --image=busybox --restart=Never -n demo-a -- /bin/sh

Inside the shell:

# DNS should work
nslookup nginx-a.demo-a.svc.cluster.local

# Access to nginx-a in demo-a should work
wget -q --timeout=5 nginx-a.demo-a.svc.cluster.local -O -

# Access to Express app should work
wget -q --timeout=5 lab1-express-svc.demo-a.svc.cluster.local:3000 -O -

# Access to demo-b should still be BLOCKED
wget -qO- --timeout=5 nginx-b.demo-b.svc.cluster.local

Expected Result:

  • DNS: works
  • nginx-a: works (NGINX welcome page)
  • lab1-express: works (Express greeting)
  • nginx-b (cross-namespace): blocked (timeout)

Type exit to leave the test pod.

docs.title: 8.5 Observe in Hubble
docs.text:
hubble observe --namespace demo-a --last 15

In the Hubble UI, demo-a should now show green flows between test pods, nginx-a, and lab1-express. Attempts to reach demo-b still appear as red drops. You can filter by verdict in the UI to isolate allowed vs. ❌ traffic.

docs.title: 8.6 Review the policy stack
docs.text:
kubectl get networkpolicies -n demo-a

Expected Result:

Policy Direction Description default-deny-all Ingress/Egress Blanket deny, both directions, all pods allow-dns-egress Egress Allow DNS (port 53) to kube-system allow-nginx-a-ingress Ingress Allow ingress to nginx-a on port 80 allow-express-ingress Ingress Allow ingress to Express on port 3000 allow-http-egress Egress Allow HTTP/HTTPS/Express egress in demo-a

docs.title: 8.7 Remove the Express ingress policy
docs.text:
Note: We are intentionally removing the NetworkPolicy for the Express app's ingress here. The Express app ingress will be managed by a CiliumNetworkPolicy in Task 9, which provides both L3/L4 and L7 (HTTP-aware) filtering in a single policy. If we created a standard NetworkPolicy allowing port 3000, it would bypass Cilium's L7 proxy.

kubectl delete -f allow-express-ingress.yaml -n demo-a

docs.title: Task 9: Cilium L7 HTTP Policy (CiliumNetworkPolicy)
docs.text:
This is where Cilium goes beyond what standard Kubernetes NetworkPolicies can do. A CiliumNetworkPolicy can inspect and filter traffic at the application layer (L7), allowing you to write rules based on HTTP methods, paths, and headers.

Why L7 policies matter: Standard NetworkPolicies only control which pods can talk to which ports (L3/L4). With Cilium L7 policies, you can say: "Allow GET requests to /health but deny POST requests to /admin." This provides much finer-grained security. Why not use both? To enforce L7 filtering, the CiliumNetworkPolicy must be the sole ingress policy for that endpoint — which is why we are removing the allow-express-ingress NetworkPolicy at the end of Task 8.

docs.title: 9.1 Create a CiliumNetworkPolicy for the Express app
docs.text:
Create l7-express-policy.yaml:

apiVersion: "cilium.io/v2"
kind: CiliumNetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: l7-express-policy
  namespace: demo-a
spec:
  endpointSelector:
    matchLabels:
      app: lab1-express
  ingress:
    - fromEndpoints:
        - matchLabels:
            k8s:io.kubernetes.pod.namespace: demo-a
      toPorts:
        - ports:
            - port: "3000"
              protocol: TCP
          rules:
            http:
              - method: "GET"
                path: "/"
              - method: "GET"
                path: "/health"

What this policy does:

  • Applies to pods with label app: lab1-express
  • Only allows ingress from pods in demo-a
  • Only allows GET requests to / and /health on port 3000
  • Any other HTTP method (POST, PUT, DELETE) or path will be ❌

Apply:

kubectl apply -f l7-express-policy.yaml

docs.title: 9.2 Verify the CiliumNetworkPolicy
docs.text:
kubectl get ciliumnetworkpolicies -n demo-a
kubectl describe ciliumnetworkpolicy l7-express-policy -n demo-a

Also cnp could be used as a shorthand

Expected Result:

The policy should be listed and show the L7 HTTP rules.

docs.title: 9.3 Test allowed requests (GET / and GET /health)
docs.text:
flowchart LR subgraph demo-a["namespace: demo-a"] subgraph l7["Cilium L7 Proxy"] direction TB R1("GET / → ✅ Allow") R2("GET /health → ✅ Allow") R3("POST / → ❌ Deny") R4("GET /admin → ❌ Deny") R5("DELETE /health → ❌ Deny") end curlpod(curlpod)--Port 3000-->l7-->express(lab1-express) end classDef k8s_nodes padding: 8px,fill:#0a60ff,color:#e7e7e7, stroke:#07182d,stroke-width:2px, font-size:1.25em; classDef k8s_ns padding: 16px,stroke:#07182d,stroke-width:4px, font-size:1.1em; class curlpod,express,R1,R2,R3,R4,R5 k8s_nodes; class demo-a,l7 k8s_ns;

Cilium's L7 proxy inspects HTTP requests before they reach the application. Only GET / and GET /health pass through — all other methods and paths are ❌ with 403.

Deploy a curl pod for testing (busybox wget does not support custom HTTP methods easily):

kubectl run curlpod --rm -ti --image=curlimages/curl --restart=Never -n demo-a -- /bin/sh

Inside the curl pod:

# GET / — should succeed (200)
curl -s -o /dev/null -w "%{http_code}" http://lab1-express-svc:3000/

# GET /health — should succeed (200)
curl -s -o /dev/null -w "%{http_code}" http://lab1-express-svc:3000/health

Expected Result:

Both return 200.

docs.title: 9.4 Test ❌ requests (POST and unknown paths)
docs.text:
Still inside the curl pod:

# POST to / — should be ❌ (403)
curl -s -o /dev/null -w "%{http_code}" -X POST http://lab1-express-svc:3000/

# GET /admin — should be ❌ (403)
curl -s -o /dev/null -w "%{http_code}" http://lab1-express-svc:3000/admin

# DELETE /health — should be ❌ (403)
curl -s -o /dev/null -w "%{http_code}" -X DELETE http://lab1-express-svc:3000/health

Expected Result:

All three return 403 — Cilium's L7 proxy denies the requests before they reach the Express application.

Type exit to leave the curl pod.

This demonstrates the power of Cilium L7 policies: you can enforce application-layer security without modifying the application code.

docs.title: 9.5 Observe L7 drops in Hubble
docs.text:
hubble observe --namespace demo-a --verdict DROPPED --last 20

Expected Result:

You should see entries showing HTTP requests that were dropped by the L7 policy, including the HTTP method and path.

In the Hubble UI, select demo-a — you should see the lab1-express service with both green (allowed GET) and red (dropped POST/DELETE) flows. This is L7 visibility in action.

docs.title: Task 10: Cilium FQDN-Based Egress Policy
docs.text:
Standard Kubernetes NetworkPolicies can only filter egress by IP address or CIDR. Cilium extends this with FQDN-based egress filtering, allowing you to write rules based on domain names. This is particularly useful for controlling which external services your workloads can reach.

In this task, you will lock down demo-b so that pods can only reach google.com — all other external traffic will be ❌.

flowchart LR subgraph demo-b["namespace: demo-b"] curlpod(curlpod)--Port 53-->dns(DNS Proxy)--resolve-->kube(kube-dns) curlpod--Port 443-->fqdn{{"toFQDNs\ngoogle.com"}} fqdn--✅ Allow-->google(www.google.com) fqdn--❌ Deny-->example(example.com) end classDef k8s_nodes padding: 8px,fill:#0a60ff,color:#e7e7e7, stroke:#07182d,stroke-width:2px, font-size:1.25em; class curlpod,kube,dns,kube,fqdn,google,example k8s_nodes;

Key concept: Cilium's DNS proxy intercepts DNS queries, learns which IPs map to which domain names, and uses that mapping to enforce FQDN-based policies. Without the DNS proxy (rules: dns:), Cilium cannot associate resolved IPs with domain names and the toFQDNs rules will not work.

docs.title: 10.1 Create the FQDN egress policy
docs.text:
Create a new folder for demo-b policies:

mkdir -p ~/workspace/k8s/lab4/policies-b
cd ~/workspace/k8s/lab4/policies-b

Create l7-allow-google-egress.yaml:

apiVersion: "cilium.io/v2"
kind: CiliumNetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: l7-allow-google-egress
  namespace: demo-b
spec:
  endpointSelector: {}
  ingress:
    - {}
  egress:
    - toEndpoints:
        - matchLabels:
            k8s:io.kubernetes.pod.namespace: kube-system
            k8s-app: kube-dns
      toPorts:
        - ports:
            - port: "53"
              protocol: ANY
          rules:
            dns:
              - matchPattern: "*"
    - toFQDNs:
        - matchName: "google.com"
        - matchName: "www.google.com"
      toPorts:
        - ports:
            - port: "443"
            - port: "80"

Key elements of this policy:

  • endpointSelector: {} — applies to all pods in demo-b
  • ingress: - {} — allows all ingress (we're focusing on egress control here)
  • rules: dns: - matchPattern: "*" — critical: activates Cilium's DNS proxy so it can intercept DNS responses and populate the FQDN-to-IP cache
  • toFQDNs — only allows egress to IPs that resolved from google.com and www.google.com
  • Any egress to other domains (e.g., example.com) will be ❌
  • This is impossible with standard Kubernetes NetworkPolicies, which only support IP/CIDR selectors

Apply:

kubectl apply -f l7-allow-google-egress.yaml

docs.title: 10.2 Verify the policy
docs.text: kubectl get cnp -n demo-b

docs.title: 10.3 Test the FQDN policy
docs.text:
kubectl run curlpod --rm -ti --image=curlimages/curl --restart=Never -n demo-b -- /bin/sh

Inside the curl pod:

# google.com — should succeed
curl -4 -s -o /dev/null -w "%{http_code}" --max-time 5 https://www.google.com

# example.com — should TIMEOUT (not in the allow list)
curl -4 -s -o /dev/null -w "%{http_code}" --max-time 5 https://example.com

Note: The -4 flag forces IPv4. This avoids issues where DNS returns IPv6 addresses that may not be tracked by Cilium's FQDN cache.

Expected Result:

  • www.google.com → 200
  • example.com → timeout (connection refused or no response)

Type exit to leave the curl pod.

docs.title: 10.4 Observe in Hubble
docs.text:
hubble observe --namespace demo-b --last 15

In the Hubble UI, select demo-b. You should see flows to www.google.com as green (forwarded on port 443), and any attempt to reach other domains as red (dropped). Notice how Hubble shows the resolved FQDN in the destination identity — this confirms the DNS proxy is working.

docs.title: 10.5 Verify the FQDN cache
docs.text:
You can inspect what Cilium's DNS proxy has learned:

kubectl exec -n kube-system $(kubectl get pods -n kube-system -l k8s-app=cilium -o jsonpath='{.items[0].metadata.name}') -- cilium fqdn cache list | grep google

Expected Result:

You should see entries mapping google.com and www.google.com to their resolved IP addresses.

docs.title: Task 11: Expose the Secured App with LoadBalancer and L2 Announcements
docs.text:
Now that your workloads are secured with both L3/L4 and L7 policies, it's time to expose them to the outside world using a production-like access model.

In Lab 2, you used NodePort to expose services externally. Cilium supports a more robust approach using LoadBalancer services with L2 announcements. This allows Cilium to respond to ARP requests on the local network and assign external IPs to services — similar to MetalLB in L2 mode, but built directly into Cilium.

flowchart LR host("Host / Jumphost") host--"curl http://LB_IP"-->lb["LoadBalancer Service (External IP)"] subgraph kind["KinD Cluster"] lb-->cilium{{"Cilium L2 ARP Response"}} cilium-->l7("L7 Proxy GET only") l7-->express(lab1-express Port 3000) end classDef k8s_nodes padding: 8px,fill:#0a60ff,color:#e7e7e7, stroke:#07182d,stroke-width:2px, font-size:1.25em; classDef k8s_ns padding: 16px,stroke:#07182d,stroke-width:4px, font-size:1.1em; class host,lb,cilium,l7,express k8s_nodes; class kind k8s_ns;

The full traffic path: external request → L2-announced LoadBalancer IP → Cilium → L3/L4 NetworkPolicy check → L7 HTTP policy check → Express app. Your security policies remain enforced even for externally-originated traffic.

docs.title: 11.1 Create a CiliumL2AnnouncementPolicy
docs.text:
Create a new folder named lb in ~/workspace/k8s/lab4/

mkdir -p ~/workspace/k8s/lab4/lb
cd ~/workspace/k8s/lab4/lb

Create l2-announcement.yaml:

apiVersion: "cilium.io/v2alpha1"
kind: CiliumL2AnnouncementPolicy
metadata:
  name: default-l2-policy
spec:
  interfaces:
    - ^eth[0-9]+
  externalIPs: true
  loadBalancerIPs: true

Apply:

kubectl apply -f l2-announcement.yaml

docs.title: 11.2 Create a CiliumLoadBalancerIPPool
docs.text:
Create lb-ip-pool.yaml:

apiVersion: "cilium.io/v2alpha1"
kind: CiliumLoadBalancerIPPool
metadata:
  name: default-pool
spec:
  blocks:
    - cidr: "172.18.255.200/28"

Note: The 172.18.0.0/16 subnet is the default Docker bridge network used by KinD. We pick a small range (172.18.255.200/28) within it for LoadBalancer IPs to avoid conflicts. You can verify your KinD network with docker network inspect kind.

Apply:

kubectl apply -f lb-ip-pool.yaml

docs.title: 11.3 Update the CiliumNetworkPolicy to allow external ingress
docs.text:
The current l7-express-policy only permits ingress from pods within demo-a. LoadBalancer traffic originates from outside the cluster, so we need to update the policy to also allow external sources — while still enforcing L7 HTTP rules.

Update l7-express-policy.yaml with an additional ingress rule:

apiVersion: "cilium.io/v2"
kind: CiliumNetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: l7-express-policy
  namespace: demo-a
spec:
  endpointSelector:
    matchLabels:
      app: lab1-express
  ingress:
    - fromEndpoints:
        - matchLabels:
            k8s:io.kubernetes.pod.namespace: demo-a
      toPorts:
        - ports:
            - port: "3000"
              protocol: TCP
          rules:
            http:
              - method: "GET"
                path: "/"
              - method: "GET"
                path: "/health"
    - fromEntities:
        - world
      toPorts:
        - ports:
            - port: "3000"
              protocol: TCP
          rules:
            http:
              - method: "GET"
                path: "/"
              - method: "GET"
                path: "/health"

What changed: A second ingress rule was added using fromEntities: world, which matches traffic originating from outside the cluster (e.g., via the LoadBalancer). The same L7 HTTP rules apply — only GET / and GET /health are allowed. This keeps all Express ingress under a single CiliumNetworkPolicy with consistent L7 enforcement.

Apply:

kubectl apply -f l7-express-policy.yaml

docs.title: 11.4 Create a LoadBalancer Service for the Express app
docs.text:
Create express-lb-svc.yaml:

apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: lab1-express-lb
  namespace: demo-a
spec:
  selector:
    app: lab1-express
  type: LoadBalancer
  ports:
    - port: 80
      targetPort: 3000

Apply:

kubectl apply -f express-lb-svc.yaml

docs.title: 11.5 Verify the LoadBalancer IP is assigned
docs.text:
kubectl get svc lab1-express-lb -n demo-a

Expected Result:

The EXTERNAL-IP column should show an IP from the 172.18.255.200/28 pool (e.g., 172.18.255.200). Use it to test from the terminal using curl.

NAME              TYPE           CLUSTER-IP     EXTERNAL-IP      PORT(S)        AGE
lab1-express-lb   LoadBalancer   10.96.x.x      172.18.255.200   80:3xxxx/TCP   10s

docs.title: 11.6 Test connectivity via the LoadBalancer IP
docs.text:
Use the EXTERNAL-IP from the kubectl get svc ... output to test with curl:

kubectl get svc lab1-express-lb -n demo-a

curl http://172.18.255.200
curl http://172.18.255.200/health

Expected Result:

Hello from Express!
Hostname: lab1-express-xxxxx
Time: ...

docs.title: 11.7 Verify L7 policies still apply to external traffic
docs.text:
The L7 CiliumNetworkPolicy should still enforce HTTP method restrictions, even for traffic coming through the LoadBalancer. Replace <EXTERNAL-IP> with the IP from step 11.5:

# GET / — should succeed (200)
curl -s -o /dev/null -w "%{http_code}" http://<EXTERNAL-IP>/

# POST / — should be ❌ (403)
curl -s -o /dev/null -w "%{http_code}" -X POST http://<EXTERNAL-IP>/

# GET /admin — should be ❌ (403)
curl -s -o /dev/null -w "%{http_code}" http://<EXTERNAL-IP>/admin

Expected Result:

  • GET / → 200
  • POST / → 403
  • GET /admin → 403

This confirms that your L7 security policies are enforced end-to-end, from external LoadBalancer access all the way down to the application layer.

docs.title: 11.8 Observe in Hubble
docs.text:
hubble observe --namespace demo-a --last 15

In the Hubble UI, look for traffic coming from the world identity into lab1-express. You should see both forwarded (GET) and dropped (POST) flows — proving that L7 enforcement works for external traffic through the LoadBalancer.

docs.title: Task 12: Observe the Full Picture in Hubble UI
docs.text: Now that you have active workloads, policies, FQDN filtering, and external access, take a moment to explore the full Hubble UI.

docs.title: 12.1 Ensure port-forwarding is active
docs.text:
If it is not already running:

kubectl port-forward -n kube-system svc/hubble-ui 12000:80 --address 0.0.0.0 &

docs.title: 12.2 Open Hubble UI
docs.text:
From the Jumphost browser, navigate to:

http://198.18.128.50:12000

docs.title: 12.3 Explore the Service Maps
docs.text:
1. Select the demo-a namespace — you should see nginx-a, lab1-express, and connections from world (LoadBalancer traffic)
  2. Select the demo-b namespace — you should see flows to www.google.com and dropped flows to other domains
  3. Try filtering by verdict (forwarded vs. dropped) to isolate policy effects
  4. Run some test pods and curl commands while watching to see real-time updates

Tip: Hubble is not just a visualization tool — in production, it becomes your primary tool for debugging network policy issues. The patterns you've practiced here (observe by namespace, filter by verdict, check FQDN resolution) are the same ones you'll use to troubleshoot real connectivity problems.

docs.title: Task 13: Cleanup
docs.text: This will remove the config you create during the lab. You can keep the files in your local machine for future reference

docs.title: 13.1 Delete namespaces and policies
docs.text: kubectl delete ns demo-a demo-b

docs.title: 13.2 Delete the L2 and LoadBalancer resources
docs.text:
cd ~/workspace/k8s/lab4
kubectl delete -f lb/l2-announcement.yaml
kubectl delete -f lb/lb-ip-pool.yaml

docs.title: 13.3 Destroy the KinD cluster
docs.text: kind delete cluster --name my-k8s

docs.title: 13.4 Confirm cleanup
docs.text:
kind get clusters

Expected Result:

No clusters listed.

docs.title: Lab Completion
docs.text:
Congratulations! You have completed Lab 4!

  • You created a KinD cluster without CNI and without kube-proxy, and installed Cilium as a full kube-proxy replacement using Helm.

  • You built a complete L3/L4 NetworkPolicy stack (deny-all, allow-dns, allow-http) and extended it with Cilium's L7 HTTP-aware CiliumNetworkPolicy.

  • You applied FQDN-based egress policies to control which external domains workloads can reach — a capability unique to Cilium and impossible with standard Kubernetes NetworkPolicies.

  • You configured LoadBalancer services with L2 announcements — a production-like approach to external service access — and verified that L7 policies are enforced end-to-end, even for external traffic.

  • You used Hubble CLI and UI throughout the lab to observe, verify, and debug policy enforcement in real time — building the skills needed to troubleshoot network issues in production.

  • You now understand how Cilium goes beyond traditional CNIs by combining eBPF-powered networking, deep observability, and application-layer security.

docs.title: Scenario 1: Containerizing Apps
docs.text:
In this lab, you will build and containerize a simple web application that will be reused in later Kubernetes exercises. You will start by writing a lightweight HTTP service, package it into a Docker image, and deploy it using both docker run and Docker Compose.

The goal is to help you understand the complete container lifecycle and how a containerized workload is exposed to the outside world—first with Docker, and later with Kubernetes Services, Pods, and Deployments.

docs.title: Goals
docs.text:
By the end of this lab, you will be able to:

  • Build a basic web application (ExpressJS)
  • Create a Dockerfile to containerize the application
  • Build and run a container image locally
  • Deploy the same workload with Docker Compose
  • Preserve artifacts for reuse in Kubernetes Labs (Lab 2 and beyond)

docs.title: Estimated Completion
docs.text: 1 Hr

docs.title: Tasks
docs.text:
flowchart LR   code("Write App
(server.js)")-->docker("Containerize
(Dockerfile)")   docker-->build("Build Image
(docker build)")   build-->run("Run Container
(docker run)")   build-->compose("Deploy
(docker compose)")   run-->test("Test
(curl)")   compose-->test    classDef k8s_nodes padding: 8px,fill:#0a60ff,color:#e7e7e7, stroke:#07182d,stroke-width:2px, font-size:1.25em;    class code,docker,build,run,compose,test k8s_nodes;

docs.title: Task 1: Write a Basic Web Service (ExpressJS)
docs.text: In this task, you will create a minimal HTTP service that can later be deployed as a Kubernetes Pod and Deployment.

docs.title: 1.1 Create the Application folder
docs.text:
cd ~/workspace
mkdir -p html5/lab1-express
cd html5/lab1-express

docs.title: 1.2 Initialize the Node.JS project
docs.text:
npm init -y

npm install express

Expected Result:

A package.json file is created and Express is installed under node_modules/

docs.title: 1.3 Create the application server
docs.text:
In the vscode server in chrome, create a file named server.js

const express = require("express");
const os = require("os");

const app = express();
const port = process.env.PORT || 3000;

app.get("/", (_req, res) => {
  res
    .type("text/plain")
    .send(
      `Hello from Express!\n` +
        `Hostname: ${os.hostname()}\n` +
        `Time: ${new Date().toISOString()}\n`,
    );
});

app.get("/health", (_req, res) => {
  res.json({ status: "ok" });
});

app.listen(port, () => {
  console.log(`App listening on port ${port}`);
});

docs.title: 1.4 Run a quick local test (optional)
docs.text:
node server.js

In code-server open a second terminal,

run:

curl http://localhost:3000

curl http://localhost:3000/health

Expected Result:

  • / returns a greeting and hostname

  • /health returns JSON:
{ "status": "ok" }

Stop the app with CTRL+C

docs.title: Task 2: Containerize the App (Create a Dockerfile)
docs.text:
Now you will define the container runtime environment for this service.

graph TB subgraph Dockerfile code(Code) deps(Dependencies) end classDef k8s_nodes padding: 8px,fill:#0a60ff,color:#e7e7e7, stroke:#07182d,stroke-width:2px, font-size:1.25em; class code,deps k8s_nodes;

docs.title: 2.1 Create the Dockerfile
docs.text:
In the same directory, create Dockerfile

FROM node:20-alpine

WORKDIR /app

COPY package*.json ./
RUN npm i

COPY server.js ./

ENV PORT=3000
EXPOSE 3000

CMD ["node", "server.js"]

Expected Result:

  • server.js
  • package.json
  • Dockerfile

docs.title: Task 3: Build the Container Image
docs.text:
You will now build the Docker image that packages the application.

Open a terminal in the code server

Run:

docker build -t lab1-express:1.0 .

Note: pay attention to the . (dot) at the end

Verify the image:

docker images | grep lab1-express

Expected Result:

lab1-express 1.0 ...

docs.title: Task 4: Deploy with docker run
docs.text:
Next, you will run the container and expose it externally using port mapping.

flowchart LR client("Client curl localhost:8080")--connect to -->hostport(("Host Port 8080")) hostport-- map to -->containerport{{"Container Port 3000"}} containerport--expose-->app("Express App server.js") classDef k8s_nodes padding: 8px,fill:#0a60ff,color:#e7e7e7, stroke:#07182d,stroke-width:2px, font-size:1.25em; class client,hostport,containerport,app k8s_nodes;

docs.title: 4.1 Start the container
docs.text:
docker run --name lab1-express -d -p 8080:3000 lab1-express:1.0

This maps port 3000 inside the container to port 8080 on the host, which is the same concept Kubernetes Services will provide later.

docs.title: 4.2 Validate connectivity
docs.text:
curl http://localhost:8080
curl http://localhost:8080/health

Expected Result:

  • The app is reachable on port 8080
  • You receive a response similar to:

docs.title: Task 5: Deploy with Docker Compose
docs.text: Docker Compose provides a declarative way to run containerized workloads, similar in spirit to Kubernetes manifests.

docs.title: 5.1 Create a Compose file
docs.text:
Create a compose.yaml:

services:
  express:
    image: lab1-express:1.0
    container_name: lab1-express-compose
    ports:
      - "8081:3000"
    environment:
      - PORT=3000

docs.title: 5.2 Start the service
docs.text:
docker compose up -d

docker compose ps

docs.title: 5.3 Validate access
docs.text:
curl http://localhost:8081

Expected Results:

The application is now reachable via Compose on port 8081.

docs.title: Task 6: Cleanup (Stop Workloads, Preserve Artifacts)
docs.text: This lab’s application and images will be reused in Lab 2 (Kubernetes Pods and Deployments). Cleanup should remove running containers, but must not delete source code or images.

docs.title: 6.1 Stop Compose workloads
docs.text: docker compose down

docs.title: 6.2 Remove the standalone container
docs.text: docker rm -f lab1-express 2>/dev/null || true

docs.title: 6.3 Confirm no containers remain
docs.text:
docker ps | grep lab1-express
docker image ls

Expected Result:

No running Express containers remain, but the image and files are preserved.

docs.title: Reuse in Lab 2 (Important)
docs.text:
The following artifacts created in Lab 1 will be reused later:

  • Application source: ~/workspace/html5/lab1-express/server.js
  • Container definition: ~/workspace/html5/lab1-express/Dockerfile
  • Built image: lab1-express:1.0

In Lab 2, you will redeploy this same workload as:

  • A k8s Pod
  • A k8s Deployment
  • Exposed externally via k8s Service

docs.title: Optional Add-On: Static Website with NGINX
docs.text:
To demonstrate a front-end style workload, you may also containerize a static HTML site served by NGINX.

This is optional, but useful for understanding that Kubernetes supports both:

  • API microservices (Express/FastAPI)
  • Static web frontends (NGINX)

docs.title: Add-On Task: Build a Static Site Container
A.1 Create a static site directory
docs.text:
cd ~/workspace/html5
mkdir -p lab1-nginx-static
cd lab1-nginx-static

docs.title: A.2 Create an index.html
docs.text:
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html>
  <head>
    <title>Lab 1 Static Site</title>
  </head>
  <body>
    <h1>Hello from NGINX!</h1>
    <p>This is a static site running inside a container.</p>
  </body>
</html>

docs.title: A.3 Create the Dockerfile
docs.text:
FROM nginx:alpine
COPY index.html /usr/share/nginx/html/index.html
EXPOSE 80

docs.title: A.4 Build and run
docs.text:
docker build -t lab1-static:1.0 .
docker run --name lab1-static -d -p 8082:80 lab1-static:1.0

Test:

curl http://localhost:8082

Expected Result:

<h1>Hello from NGINX!</h1>

docs.title: A.5 Delete Container
docs.text: docker rm -f lab1-static

docs.title: A.6 Challenge
docs.text:
Create a Docker Compose file for this nginx container and publish it on port 8083.

  • The service should start with docker compose up -d
  • The page should be reachable at http://localhost:8083

Expected Result

Running curl http://localhost:8083 returns the static HTML page.

Hint:

  • Your compose file will need an nginx service with a ports: mapping of "8083:80"
  • Use the image you created in step A.4
  • None environmental variables are required

docs.title: Lab Completion
docs.text:
Congratulations! You have completed Lab 1.

You now have a containerized workload that will serve as the foundation for Kubernetes deployments in the next lab.

docs.title: Scenario 3: Network Isolation with Calico in Kubernetes
docs.text:
In this lab, you will install the Calico CNI into the KinD cluster and learn how to use Kubernetes NetworkPolicies to control pod-to-pod traffic. Unlike a default Kubernetes installation, which allows all pods to communicate freely, Calico enforces network policy rules so that only permitted traffic flows are allowed.

You will deploy a simple application and then incrementally lock down traffic between workloads using NetworkPolicies. By the end of the lab, you will understand how Calico implements network policies and how workloads can be isolated in a Kubernetes cluster.

docs.title: Goals
docs.text:
By the end of this lab, you will be able to:

  • Install Calico CNI into an existing KinD cluster
  • Deploy sample workloads for traffic control demonstrations
  • Apply Kubernetes NetworkPolicies to restrict ingress and egress
  • Validate that traffic is allowed or denied based on policy rules

docs.title: Estimated Completion
docs.text: 1 hr

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
Before starting this lab, you should have:

  • Completed Lab 2 and have a running KinD cluster (my-k8s)
  • Calico will be installed against this existing cluster
  • Working manifests in: ~/workspace/k8s/lab3/

docs.title: Task 1: Install Calico CNI
docs.text: In this task, you will install Calico into your KinD cluster to enable Kubernetes NetworkPolicy enforcement.

docs.title: 1.1 Create a new KinD cluster
docs.text:
Go to ~/workspace/kind and make a new folder lab3

mkdir -p  ~/workspace/kind/lab3
cd ~/workspace/kind/lab3

Create a kind-cni-config.yml

kind: Cluster
apiVersion: kind.x-k8s.io/v1alpha4
nodes:
  - role: control-plane
    extraPortMappings:
      -containerPort: 30000
       hostPort: 30000
  - role: worker
  - role: worker
networking:
  disableDefaultCNI: true
  podSubnet: 192.168.0.0/16

Note: disableDefaultCNI config.

docs.title: 1.2 Create the new KinD Cluster
docs.text:
kind create cluster --config kind-cni-config.yml --name my-k8s

Result:

docs.title: 1.3 Verify KinD Cluster is Not Ready
docs.text:
kubectl get nodes -o wide

Expected Result:

Cluster will be in Not Ready until CNI is installed

docs.title: 1.4 Create the manifest directory
docs.text:
mkdir -p ~/workspace/k8s/lab3/calico
cd ~/workspace/k8s/lab3/calico

docs.title: 1.5 Fetch and apply the Calico installation manifest
docs.text:
kubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/projectcalico/calico/v3.31.3/manifests/calico.yaml

Expected Result:

docs.title: 1.6 Verify the Calico pods are running
docs.text:
kubectl get pods -A

Expected Result:

All Calico pods should be in Running status:

Also the cluster should be ready

docs.title: Task 2: Deploy Sample Workloads
docs.text:
In this task, you will deploy two simple NGINX pods in separate namespaces to demonstrate traffic control.

flowchart TD subgraph node["Node Worker"] subgraph demo-b["namespace: demo-b"] pod-b(nginx-b) end subgraph demo-a["namespace: demo-a"] pod-a(nginx-a) tester-a(tester-a) end end classDef k8s_nodes padding: 8px,fill:#0a60ff,color:#e7e7e7, stroke:#07182d,stroke-width:2px, font-size:1.25em; classDef k8s_ns padding: 16px,stroke:#07182d,stroke-width:4px, font-size:1.1em; class pod-b,pod-a,tester-a k8s_nodes; class demo-a,demo-b k8s_ns;

docs.title: 2.1 Create namespaces
docs.text:
Namespace is a mechanism for isolating and organizing groups of resources (like Pods, Services, and Deployments) within a single physical cluster, effectively creating "virtual clusters". They are crucial for multi-tenant environments where multiple teams or projects share the same infrastructure without interfering with each other.

Run:

kubectl create ns demo-a
kubectl create ns demo-b

Apply Labels to be used as selectors in the policy

kubectl label ns demo-a name=demo-a
kubectl label ns demo-b name=demo-b

docs.title: 2.2 Deploy NGINX in each namespace
docs.text:
Here we are going to use kubectl cli to create a deployment, which is useful for quick testing. For production is always a best practice to create manifest

Run for namespace demo-a:

kubectl create deployment nginx-a --image=nginx -n demo-a
kubectl get deployment nginx-a -n demo-a
kubectl describe deployment nginx-a -n demo-a

Create a Service by exposing the POD with a ClusterIP

kubectl expose deployment nginx-a --port=80 -n demo-a

Run for namespace demo-b:

kubectl create deployment nginx-b --image=nginx -n demo-b
kubectl expose deployment nginx-b --port=80 -n demo-b

Expected Result:

Two deployments and services exist

kubectl get svc -n demo-a
kubectl get svc -n demo-b

Service for nginx-a

Service for nginx-b

Both should show a ClusterIP service on port 80.

docs.title: Task 3: Confirm Default Connectivity
docs.text:
Before applying any network policies, Kubernetes allows all pod-to-pod traffic by default.

Note: By default Kubernetes allows all traffic unless a NetworkPolicy is applied. Network policies do not apply automatically — the CNI must support them. Calico is one such CNI that implements them.

docs.title: 3.1 Create a busybox test pod
docs.text:
In demo-a:

kubectl run busybox --rm -ti --image=busybox --restart=Never -n demo-a -- /bin/sh

busy-box is an example of an ephemeral workload. --rm flag means once the interactive session (-ti flag) finish, the pod will be destroyed right next.

Inside the busybox shell:

wget -q --timeout=5 nginx-b.demo-b.svc.cluster.local -O -

Note: the FQDN syntax nginx-b.demo-b.svc.cluster.local to call the resource inside the Cluster. The standard format is <resource-name>.<namespace-name>.<resource-type>.<cluster-domain>.

Expected Result:

The NGINX welcome HTML is returned — indicating cross-namespace connectivity.

Finally, run exit from the busy box, the workload will be deleted from Namespace.

docs.title: Native k8s Network Policies
docs.text:
Network policy is a resource that defines: How pods in one namespace can communicate with pods in another namespace and sometimes with external traffic.

In this section you are going to build several policies applying Zero-Trust and best practices.

flowchart TB subgraph demo-a["namespace: demo-a"] subgraph policies["Network Policies"] P1("🔴 deny-all podSelector: {} Ingress + Egress") P2("🟢 allow-dns-egress podSelector: {} Egress → :53") P3("🔵 allow-nginx-a-ingress podSelector: nginx-a Ingress ← :80") P4("🟢 allow-http-egress podSelector: busybox Egress → :80") end end classDef k8s_policy_node padding: 16px,stroke:#07182d,stroke-width:2px, font-size:1.5em; classDef k8s_ns padding: 24px,stroke:#07182d,stroke-width:4px,font-size:1.25em; class P1,P2,P3,P4 k8s_policy_node; class demo-a,policies k8s_ns;

If you want to control traffic flow at the IP address or port level (OSI layer 3 or 4), NetworkPolicies allow you to specify rules for traffic flow within your cluster, and also between Pods and the outside world. Your cluster must use a network plugin that supports NetworkPolicy enforcement.

Here a table with some of the CNI supporting Network Policies:

CNI Plugin Policy Support Key Features Calico Full High performance, BGP routing, supports NetworkPolicy and advanced GlobalNetworkPolicy. Cilium Full Uses eBPF for high-performance filtering, supports L7 (HTTP/gRPC) policies. Weave Net Full Easy to install, supports network policies. Amazon VPC CNI Supported Enables Kubernetes network policies directly on AWS VPC networking. Azure CNI Supported Supports FQDN filtering and standard NetworkPolicy. OpenShift CNI Supported Built-in support for OpenShift clusters.

docs.title: Network Policy Stacking in k8s
docs.text:
When multiple NetworkPolicy resources apply to the same pod, Kubernetes evaluates the union of allowed traffic across all policies. This means for any given direction (ingress or egress), if any policy allows the flow, it is permitted. Conversely, anything not allowed by at least one rule is blocked.

All the tasks in this section will show you how the stacking process works.

docs.title: Task 4: Apply a Default Deny Policy
docs.text:
Now you will restrict traffic so that only explicitly allowed connections are permitted. This lab will create a “default deny” policy per namespace.

deny-all baseline policies (recommended starting point) is a best practice to apply these first, then layer the specific allows above. Without these, pods with no policy default to allow-all. |

docs.title: 4.1 Create a deny-all ingress/egress policy in demo-a
docs.text:
Create a new policies folder

mkdir -p ~/workspace/k8s/lab3/policies

Create deny-all-a.yaml:

apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: default-deny-all
  namespace: demo-a
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  policyTypes:
    - Ingress
    - Egress

Apply it:

kubectl apply -f deny-all-a.yaml -n demo-a

Verify the policy with:

kubectl describe networkpolicy deny-all -n demo-a

docs.title: 4.2 Test connectivity after default-deny
docs.text:
Run another busybox in demo-a in a different terminal:

kubectl run testpod --rm -ti --image=busybox --restart=Never -n demo-a -- /bin/sh

Then inside the shell:

nslookup nginx-b.demo-b.svc.cluster.local
wget -qO- --timeout=5 nginx-b.demo-b.svc.cluster.local

Expected Result:

The request should fail — demonstrating that all traffic is blocked unless explicitly allowed.

The result shows that also the DNS resolution is failing, due to the forbidden egress traffic outside the namespace demo-a

docs.title: Task 5: Allow Egress to DNS Service
docs.text: Kubernetes namespace kube-system host the DNS service for the cluster usinf CoreDNS. After the deny-all policy, this traffic is also affected so a explicit rule allowing it is required.

docs.title: 5.1 Create an allow DNS policy
docs.text:
Create allow-dns-egress.yaml

apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: allow-dns-egress
  namespace: demo-a
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  policyTypes:
    - Egress
  egress:
    - to:
        - namespaceSelector:
            matchLabels:
              kubernetes.io/metadata.name: kube-system
      ports:
        - protocol: UDP
          port: 53
        - protocol: TCP
          port: 53

To get the kubernetes.io/metadata.name: kube-system label you can use the following cli command kubectl get namespace kube-system --show-labels. This shows the system label asigned to the resource by the cluster.

Apply the policy

kubectl apply -f allow-dns-egress.yaml -n demo-a

Check the Network Policy stacking with kubectl describe networkpolicies -n demo-a

docs.title: 5.2 Validate egress to DNS allowance
docs.text:
kubectl run testpod --rm -ti --image=busybox --restart=Never -n demo-a -- /bin/sh

Then run

nslookup nginx-a.demo-a.svc.cluster.local

Expected Result:

The name resolution output

docs.title: Challenge
docs.text:
1. What is the response for the wget -qO- nginx-a.demo-a.svc.cluster.local with this policy?

  2. What is the service ClusterIP response for the nginx-b.demo-b.svc.cluster.local?

docs.title: Task 6: Allow Ingress to Specific Service
docs.text: Next you will allow traffic from pods in demo-a to the nginx-a service only.

docs.title: 6.1 Create an allow policy
docs.text:
Create allow-nginx-a.yaml:

apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: allow-nginx-a
  namespace: demo-a
spec:
  podSelector:
    matchLabels:
      app: nginx-a
  policyTypes:
    - Ingress
  ingress:
    - from:
        - namespaceSelector:
            matchLabels:
              kubernetes.io/metadata.name: demo-a
      ports:
        - protocol: TCP
          port: 80

In the podSelector key it is a match for the app label to identify the Pod. You can verify with

   kubectl get pods -l app=nginx-a -n demo-a

Apply the policy

kubectl apply -f allow-nginx-a.yaml -n demo-a

Verify the policy

kubectl describe networkpolicy allow-nginx-a -n demo-a

docs.title: 6.2 Validate ingress allowance
docs.text:
Run another busybox in demo-a in a terminal:

kubectl run testpod --rm -ti --image=busybox --restart=Never -n demo-a -- /bin/sh

Within the demo-a busybox shell:

wget -q --timeout=5 nginx-a.demo-a.svc.cluster.local -O -

Expected Result:

The NGINX will fail. Why?

The reason is because your test pod is also in demo-a, so it's also under the deny-all policy.

That means it needs an explicit egress rule to port 80 to be able to make the HTTP request, otherwise the outbound connection is dropped before it even reaches nginx-a

flowchart LR subgraph demo-a["namespace: demo-a"] testpod(Testpod A)--Egress Port 53-->P1((✅))--DNS-->kube(kube-dns) testpod(Testpod A)--Egress Port 80-->P2((❌))-->nginxa(nginx-a) end subgraph demo-b["namespace: demo-b"] nginxb(nginx-b) end testpod(Testpod A)--Egress Port 80-->P3((❌))-->nginxb(nginx-b) classDef policy_deny color:#fff,stroke:#EB4651,stroke-width:2px; classDef policy_allow color:#fff,stroke:#45991f,stroke-width:2px; classDef k8s_nodes padding: 8px,fill:#0a60ff,color:#e7e7e7, stroke:#07182d,stroke-width:2px, font-size:1.25em; class P1,P2,P3 policy_deny; class P1 policy_allow; class testpod,nginxb,nginxa,kube k8s_nodes;

docs.title: 6.3 Reconfirm cross-namespace blocking
docs.text:
Inside the same demo-a test pod:

wget -qO- --timeout=5 nginx-b.demo-b.svc.cluster.local -

Expected Result: The request is still blocked — because no policy allows it.

docs.title: 6.4 Create an allow http egress policy
docs.text:
Create a new file allow-http-egress.yaml:

apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: allow-http-egress
  namespace: demo-a
spec:
  podSelector: {} # applies to all pods in demo-a, or use matchLabels: app: busybox to scope it down
  policyTypes:
    - Egress
  egress:
    - to:
        - namespaceSelector:
            matchLabels:
              name: demo-a # only allow HTTP egress within demo-a
      ports:
        - protocol: TCP
          port: 80
        - protocol: TCP
          port: 443

Apply the policy

kubectl apply -f allow-http-egress.yaml -n demo-a

Verify the policy

kubectl describe networkpolicy allow-nginx-a -n demo-a

docs.title: 6.5 Validate http egress allowance
docs.text:
Run another busybox in demo-a in a terminal:

kubectl run testpod --rm -ti --image=busybox --restart=Never -n demo-a -- /bin/sh

Within the demo-a busybox shell:

wget -q --timeout=5 nginx-a.demo-a.svc.cluster.local -O -

Expected Result:

Traffic works!

flowchart LR subgraph demo-a["namespace: demo-a"] testpod(Testpod A)--Egress Port 53-->P1((✅))--DNS-->kube(kube-dns) testpod(Testpod A)--Egress Port 80-->P2((✅))--Ingress Port 80-->nginxa(nginx-a) end subgraph demo-b["namespace: demo-b"] nginxb(nginx-b) end testpod(Testpod A)--Egress Port 80-->P3((❌))-->nginxb(nginx-b) classDef policy_deny color:#fff,stroke:#EB4651,stroke-width:2px; classDef policy_allow color:#fff,stroke:#45991f,stroke-width:2px; classDef k8s_nodes padding: 8px,fill:#0a60ff,color:#e7e7e7, stroke:#07182d,stroke-width:2px, font-size:1.25em; class P3 policy_deny; class P1,P2 policy_allow; class testpod,nginxb,nginxa,kube k8s_nodes;

docs.title: 6.6 Final Policy Set
docs.text:
Run

 kubectl get networkpolicies -n demo-a

Network Policy Summary:

Policy Direction Description default- deny-all Ingress/Egress blanket deny, both directions, all pods allow-dns-egress Egress egress only, all pods, port 53 allow-http-egress Egress HTTP/HTTPS to nginx-a pod allow-nginx-a Ingress allow ingress to port 80 from ns demo-a

Each policy has a single responsibility, a descriptive name that tells you exactly what it does, and touches only one direction. When something breaks in the future — and it will — you'll thank yourself for keeping them clean and separate.

flowchart LR subgraph demo-a["namespace: demo-a"] testpod(Testpod A)--Egress Port 53-->P1((✅))--DNS-->kube(kube-dns) testpod(Testpod A)--Egress Port 80-->P2((✅))--Ingress Port 80-->nginxa(nginx-a) end subgraph demo-b["namespace: demo-b"] nginxb(nginx-b) end testpod(Testpod A)--Egress Port 80-->P3((❌))-->nginxb(nginx-b) classDef policy_deny color:#fff,stroke:#EB4651,stroke-width:2px; classDef policy_allow color:#fff,stroke:#45991f,stroke-width:2px; classDef k8s_nodes padding: 8px,fill:#0a60ff,color:#e7e7e7, stroke:#07182d,stroke-width:2px, font-size:1.25em; class P3 policy_deny; class P1,P2 policy_allow; class testpod,nginxb,nginxa,kube k8s_nodes;

docs.title: Task 7: Cleanup
docs.text:
This will remove the config you create during the lab. You can keep the files in your local machine for future reference

Remove the sample network policies and test namespaces.

kubectl delete ns demo-a demo-b

Expected Result:

Both namespaces and all policies are deleted, returning the cluster to its previous state.

Remove the kind cluster

kind delete cluster --name my-k8s

Expected Result:

Cluster is removed

docs.title: Lab Completion
docs.text:
Congratulations! You have completed Lab 3

  • You installed Calico networking, deployed workloads, and implemented Kubernetes L3/L4 Native NetworkPolicies to secure traffic between pods.

  • You apply best practices for Network Policy. Explicit Policy for Ingress and Egress where created and tested.

  • You now understand how traffic flows by default and how to lock it down using policy rules.

docs.title: Scenario 2: Deploying Containerized Apps in Kubernetes (KinD)
docs.text:
In this lab, you will take the containerized application built in Lab 1 and deploy it into a Kubernetes cluster running locally with KinD (Kubernetes-in-Docker). You will learn how Kubernetes runs container workloads as Pods, how Deployments provide scalability and lifecycle management, and how Services expose applications outside the cluster.

The goal of this lab is to connect the Docker access model you learned previously (docker run -p ...) with the Kubernetes equivalent using Pods, Deployments, and Services.

docs.title: Goals
docs.text:
By the end of this lab, you will be able to:

  • Create a multi-node KinD Kubernetes cluster using a configuration file
  • Understand how NodePort access works in KinD through port mappings
  • Verify access to a running KinD Kubernetes cluster
  • Load a locally built Docker image into KinD without pushing to a registry
  • Deploy the Lab 1 Express application as a Pod
  • Redeploy the same application as a Deployment
  • Expose the application externally using a NodePort Service
  • Understand how Kubernetes networking maps to Docker port publishing

docs.title: Estimated Completion
docs.text: 1 Hr

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
Before starting, ensure you have completed Lab 1 and that the following artifact exists:

  • Docker image: lab1-express:1.0

Verify locally:

docker images | grep lab1-express

Expected Results

lab1-express   1.0   ...

docs.title: Workspace
docs.text:
Kubernetes manifests for this lab will be stored under:

 ~/workspace/k8s/lab2/

KinD cluster configuration will be stored under:

 ~/workspace/kind/lab2/

docs.title: Tasks
docs.text:
flowchart TB   subgraph kind["KinD Cluster (my-k8s)"]     cp("Control Plane
(Docker Container)")     w1("Worker 1
(Docker Container)")     w2("Worker 2
(Docker Container)")   end   host("Host Machine
localhost:31100")-->|NodePort mapping|cp   cp-->w1   cp-->w2    classDef k8s_nodes padding: 8px,fill:#0a60ff,color:#e7e7e7, stroke:#07182d,stroke-width:2px, font-size:1.25em;    class host,cp,w1,w2 k8s_nodes;

docs.title: Task 1: Create a Multinode Kind Kubernetes Cluster
docs.text:
In this task, you will create the Kubernetes cluster that will be used for the remainder of the workshop.

KinD runs Kubernetes nodes as Docker containers. This makes it ideal for labs and development environments.

To support external access to applications later in this training, we will configure port mappings between the Kubernetes nodes and your host machine.

docs.title: 1.1 Create the KinD configuration folder
docs.text:
mkdir -p ~/workspace/kind/lab2
cd ~/workspace/kind/lab2

docs.title: 1.2 Create the KinD cluster configuration file
docs.text:
Create a file named kind-cluster.yaml, with the following content:

apiVersion: kind.x-k8s.io/v1alpha4
kind: Cluster

nodes:
  - role: control-plane
    extraPortMappings:
      - containerPort: 30000
        hostPort: 30000
      - containerPort: 31100
        hostPort: 31100
      - containerPort: 31200
        hostPort: 31200
      - containerPort: 31300
        hostPort: 31300
      - containerPort: 31400
        hostPort: 31400
      - containerPort: 31500
        hostPort: 31500

  - role: worker
  - role: worker

docs.title: 1.3 Understanding Port Mappings
docs.text:
In Kubernetes, applications can be exposed externally using a NodePort Service.

For example:

  • Application runs inside the Pod in port 3000
  • k8s exposes it externally via NodePort 30000

However, in KinD the Kubernetes nodes are Docker containers, so NodePorts are not automatically reachable unless we map them.

These mappings enable:

localhost:<port> → KinD node → Kubernetes Service → Pod
NodePort Host Port Used For 31100 31100 Express App 31200 31200 Future. 31300 31300 Future. 31400 31400 Future. 31500 31500 Future.

docs.title: 1.4 Create the cluster
docs.text:
kind create cluster --name my-k8s --config kind-cluster.yaml

Expected Result:

Creating cluster "my-k8s" ...
✓ Installing CNI ...
✓ Installing StorageClass ...
Set kubectl context to "kind-my-k8s"

docs.title: 1.5 Verify the cluster is running
docs.text:
kubectl get nodes -o wide

Expected Result

my-k8s-control-plane   Ready...
my-k8s-worker          Ready...
my-k8s-worker2         Ready...

docs.title: Task 2: Load the Lab 1 Container Image into KinD
docs.text:
One advantage of KinD is that you can deploy local images directly into the cluster without pushing to container registry.

A container registry is a centralized, secure storage system for container images, acting as a repository to store, manage, and distribute them

docs.title: 2.1 Load the container image into the cluster
docs.text:
kind load docker-image lab1-express:1.0 --name my-k8s

Expected Result:

docs.title: Task 3: Deploy the App as a Pod
docs.text:
A Pod is the simplest way Kubernetes runs a container workload.

flowchart TD subgraph cluster["KinD Cluster"] subgraph pod["Pod: lab1-express-pod"] container{{"Container\nlab1-express:1.0\nPort 3000"}} end end classDef k8s_nodes padding: 8px,fill:#0a60ff,color:#e7e7e7, stroke:#07182d,stroke-width:2px, font-size:1.25em; classDef k8s_pods padding: 8px,fill:#02c8ff,color:#07182d, stroke:#ff9000,stroke-width:2px class container k8s_pods; class pod k8s_nodes;

docs.title: 3.1 Create a manifest folder
docs.text:
mkdir -p ~/workspace/k8s/lab2
cd ~/workspace/k8s/lab2

docs.title: 3.2 Create a Pod Manifest
docs.text:
Create pod.yaml

apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: lab1-express-pod
  labels:
    app: lab1-express
spec:
  containers:
    - name: express
      image: lab1-express:1.0
      imagePullPolicy: Never
      ports:
        - containerPort: 3000

docs.title: 3.3 Apply the Pod
docs.text: kubectl apply -f pod.yaml

docs.title: 3.4 Verify the Pod is running
docs.text:
kubectl get pods

Expected Result:

Note kubecolor is an alternative to kubectl to get a colorful output.

docs.title: Task 4: Access the Pod (Port Forwarding)
docs.text: Before introducing Services, Kubernetes allows temporary access via port forwarding.

docs.title: 4.1 Forward the Pod port locally
docs.text: kubectl port-forward pod/lab1-express-pod 8080:3000

docs.title: 4.2 Test in a second terminal
docs.text:
curl http://localhost:8080
curl http://localhost:8080/health

Expected Result:

{ "status": "ok" }

docs.title: Task 5: Deploy the App as a Deployment
docs.text:
Deployments provide scalability, rolling updates, and self-healing.

flowchart TD subgraph cluster["KinD Cluster"] deploy("Deployment lab1-express-deployment replicas: 2") deploy-->pod1("Pod 1 lab1-express:1.0") deploy-->pod2("Pod 2 lab1-express:1.0") end classDef k8s_nodes padding: 8px,fill:#0a60ff,color:#e7e7e7, stroke:#07182d,stroke-width:2px, font-size:1.25em; class deploy,pod1,pod2 k8s_nodes;

docs.title: 5.1 Create the Deployment manifest
docs.text:
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: lab1-express-deployment
spec:
  replicas: 2
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: lab1-express
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: lab1-express
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: express
          image: lab1-express:1.0
          imagePullPolicy: Never
          ports:
            - containerPort: 3000

docs.title: 5.2 Apply the Deployment
docs.text: kubectl apply -f deployment.yaml

docs.title: 5.3 Verify replicas
docs.text:
kubectl get pods -l app=lab1-express

Expected Result:

Two Pods should be running

docs.title: Task 6: Expose the Deployment with a Service (NodePort)
docs.text:
A Service provides stable access and enables external connectivity.

flowchart LR client("Client curl localhost:31100")-- connect to -->np(("NodePort :31100")) subgraph cluster["KinD Cluster"] np-- map to -->svc{{"Service lab1-express-svc Port 3000"}} svc-- expose -->pod1("Pod 1 :3000") svc-- expose -->pod2("Pod 2 :3000") end classDef k8s_nodes padding: 8px,fill:#0a60ff,color:#e7e7e7, stroke:#07182d,stroke-width:2px, font-size:1.25em; class client,np,svc,pod1,pod2 k8s_nodes;

NodePort service exposes applications externally by opening a specific port (typically 30000-32767) on every node's IP address. Traffic sent to this port on any node is routed to the service, allowing outside access to pods even if they are not on that node. It is ideal for development, testing, or simple, non-production setups.

docs.title: 6.1 Create the Service Manifest
docs.text:
Create service.yaml

apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: lab1-express-svc
spec:
  selector:
    app: lab1-express
  type: NodePort
  ports:
    - port: 3000
      targetPort: 3000
      nodePort: 31100

docs.title: 6.2 Apply the Service
docs.text: kubectl apply -f service.yaml

docs.title: 6.3 Verify the Service
docs.text:
kubectl get svc lab1-express-svc

Expected Result:

NAME               TYPE       CLUSTER-IP      EXTERNAL-IP   PORT(S)          AGE
lab1-express-svc   NodePort   10.96.161.138   <none>        3000:31100/TCP   22s

docs.title: 6.4 Access the app externally
docs.text:
curl http://localhost:31100
curl http://localhost:31100/health

Expected Result:

docs.title: Task 7: Cleanup
docs.text: This will remove the config you create during the lab. You can keep the files in your local machine for future reference

docs.title: 7.1 Delete k8s created resources
docs.text: kubectl delete -f ~/workspace/k8s/lab2/

docs.title: 7.2 Destroy the KinD Cluster
docs.text: kind delete cluster --name my-k8s

docs.title: 7.3 Confirm cleanup
docs.text:
kind get clusters

Expected Result:

docs.title: Lab Completion
docs.text:
Congratulations! You have completed Lab 2.

You have successfully created a Kubernetes cluster, deployed a containerized application using Pods and Deployments, and exposed it externally through a NodePort Service.

docs.title: Getting Started: Navigating the Infrastructure
docs.text: In this introductory lab, you will familiarize yourself with the dCloud environment and infrastructure topology used throughout the rest of the workshop.
